What to Do When You Get in a Traffic Accident? — On-Scene Handling (1)
What to do when you get in a traffic accident?
Nobody wants a traffic accident — beyond property damage, people get hurt. We'd all rather avoid it, but life doesn't always cooperate. So if you find yourself in one, I hope this post can help.

I'm not a professional. This post is based on my own situation and what I learned along the way — if there's anything wrong or off, feel free to correct me. (Also note: this is based on the legal context in Taiwan; laws and procedures may differ in your jurisdiction.)
Important up front — when the accident happens,
. Leaving can constitute "hit and run", which is a

"Hit and run" means leaving the scene without getting out to check on injuries or properly handling the situation. Even if the contact was minor, you still need to handle it properly — otherwise it counts as a hit and run.
Next, check the condition of both parties. Whether or not anyone is injured, dial
(police) — that's step one. If anyone is injured, also dial
Ambulance dispatch will also notify the police
No injuries, only property damage: call
In my case, someone was injured, so we called 119. One important concept to remember during this:
If someone is injured, don't move them. In traffic accidents, the head, neck, and spine are the most commonly injured areas, and moving them carelessly can cause secondary injury.

When the ambulance arrives, you can decide whether to ride in it — but I'd recommend going. The main reason is to "make sure you have a hospital record", because the medical record links your injuries to the accident. If you self-treat later, the doctor may not formally link your injuries to the accident, which hurts you in subsequent compensation claims.

Going in the ambulance is fine — the police will come to the hospital later, and you can give them your statement then. I didn't take the ambulance because I could go to the hospital myself. But if you self-treat, you carry the risk that the doctor may not link your injuries to the accident. I went to the hospital immediately, so the doctor did formally link my injuries.
Also: if you or the other party can't move temporarily, remember to set up warning markers so trailing vehicles don't cause a secondary collision.

One last reminder — if you have third-party liability insurance, the insurance company will send someone on scene after you report. You can coordinate with their staff to handle the rest. In my case I only had the mandatory insurance, no third-party, so I handled most of it myself.

Don't assume you can never move the vehicle after a traffic accident — that's a common misconception. Whether you can move it depends on the situation. If anyone is injured, don't move it. But if no one is injured, the vehicle is still drivable, and the accident is minor, you

In my case, there were injuries, the vehicle wasn't drivable, and the accident was moderate, so we couldn't move the vehicle — also to protect the injured party's rights. What happens if no one is hurt, the vehicle is drivable, and the accident is minor, but you don't move it?
You can actually be in violation of the "
Road Traffic Management and Penalty Act
" — i.e., obstructing traffic. If you cause significant obstruction you can be fined. So don't assume "accident = don't move" — judge based on the situation.
When moving the vehicle, definitely take photos for the record and get the other party's consent.
Wait patiently for police and ambulance
After making the initial assessments, wait for the police and ambulance. You can ask nearby shops or homes for help — water, an umbrella, etc., to help the injured.
When the police arrive, they go through:
Individual interview (interview each party about the situation)
" phase, there's one question to be especially careful about:
What was your speed at the time?
After an accident, your head is usually scrambled and you can't remember your exact speed. If you're unsure or don't know, don't just blurt out an answer. Instead, say:
I'm not sure — refer to the accident assessment result.
This isn't dodging responsibility — it's letting the objective facts speak instead of your scrambled memory.
If you randomly pick a number like
, the police will write something like this in the preliminary report:
Possibly speeding — XX road/section speed limit

Why do I know this? Because I accidentally gave a number, even though the official accident assessment later confirmed I wasn't speeding. But that single line still appears on the preliminary report, and before the assessment comes out, it can absolutely be used against you.

during the individual interview, because the transcript has legal weight. If you say something against your own interest at this stage, it'll affect what comes next — it becomes reference material for prosecutors deciding whether to indict and judges deciding whether to sentence. So be careful here.

Once the police have wrapped up their investigation, you can leave or negotiate with the other party. I'd recommend leaving each other's contact info — accidents aren't something either party wanted, and you should keep checking in.
When the police leave, you'll get the "Traffic Accident Party Registration Form" — this is
important, don't lose it. You'll need it for everything that follows:
Preliminary Traffic Accident Analysis Report (the "preliminary report")
I'd recommend making a copy of the registration form so it's easier to use later.
" app for scanning — it keeps your documents complete and accessible anywhere.
Special case: no police report, but you settle on your own
There's an extra common situation worth mentioning:
In this case, both parties can settle on their own without calling the police. But remember:
Make sure the entire process has video or written records, and exchange contact info — otherwise you risk disputes down the line.
That protects your rights. If you want to be safer, just call the police regardless — that way you also avoid being accused of hit and run later.

When an accident actually happens, everyone freaks out — so staying calm matters a lot. That's how you protect your own rights. I'll continue writing about this in stages, so this is where this post ends.
Nobody wants a traffic accident — beyond property damage, people get hurt. We'd all rather avoid it, but life doesn't always cooperate. So this post is meant to help if you ever find yourself in one.
